# MACRO REPORT — 2026-04-28
**Delivered:** 12:16 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Markets are navigating a "Super Central Bank Week" — the most consequential policy calendar of 2026 — with the Fed, BoJ, BoC, ECB, and BoE all delivering rate decisions between Wednesday and Thursday. The single most important variable for today's session is the Iran-Hormuz geopolitical risk premium embedded in energy prices: U.S.-Iran peace talks have stalled after President Trump rejected Tehran's conditional Hormuz reopening proposal, keeping Brent crude above $105/bbl and gold under pressure from hawkish rate-path repricing. Equities remain near record highs despite the ongoing energy shock, buoyed by resilient earnings expectations and a pre-trade in Magnificent Seven tech results [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/27/us-iran-peace-talks-stall-global-markets-stocks-oil-treasurys.html]. The VIX has spiked 6.83% to 19.25, signalling the market is beginning to reprice event risk ahead of Wednesday's cascade of FOMC decisions and Mag-7 earnings [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/live/stock-market-today-tuesday-april-28-225503310.html].

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

Global risk assets are caught between two powerful crosscurrents: resilient equity momentum driven by AI earnings optimism and an intensifying energy-inflation shock stemming from the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The IMF has issued explicit warnings on stagflation risks, noting that the war-driven energy price surge is threatening to embed inflation across goods and services while simultaneously dampening real economic activity [Source: https://www.icis.com/chemicals-and-the-economy/2026/04/imf-warns-on-inflation-and-recession-risks-as-iran-war-continues/]. Global visible oil inventories have declined by 255 million barrels since the conflict began and are approaching all-time lows, providing fundamental support for elevated crude prices.

The Federal Reserve convenes Wednesday in what is widely expected to be Jerome Powell's final FOMC meeting as Chair, with Kevin Warsh expected to assume the role in May after Sen. Thom Tillis dropped his confirmation block. The Fed is unanimously expected to hold rates steady at 3.50%-3.75%, where they have remained since December 2025. U.S. CPI stands at 3.32% YoY as of March 2026, with PPI MoM at 1.78%, both elevated by energy passthrough [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL]. The 10-year Treasury yield sits at 4.31% and the 2-year at 3.78%, reflecting a 53bp term premium and a market not yet fully pricing rate cuts for 2026 [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10].

Equities have posted surprising resilience: the S&P 500 closed Monday at record levels of 7,173.91, while the Nasdaq 100 sits at 27,305.68, with new intraday highs recorded on Monday [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/27/stock-market-today-live-updates.html]. The rally is being led by AI and tech ahead of Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft all reporting on Wednesday — an unprecedented same-day earnings concentration representing the core of the $650 billion AI capex supercycle [Source: https://www.marketwatch.com/story/wall-streets-super-bowl-wednesday-alphabet-amazon-microsoft-and-meta-report-along-with-powells-last-fed-meeting-88eb1710].

In Asia, the Bank of Japan held rates steady in a split vote on Tuesday, with the yen firming modestly. The hawkish dissent within the BoJ reflects growing concern that the Iran war is embedding inflation domestically. The ECB and BoE are both expected to hold on Thursday, though both may signal openness to hikes if energy inflation persists, representing a material hawkish pivot from just months ago [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/27/treasury-yields-fed-policy-week-mpc-powell.html]. Eurozone inflation expectations jumped sharply in March per an ECB survey, complicating the ECB's dovish guidance [Source: https://www.wsj.com/economy/eurozone-inflation-expectations-jumped-in-march-but-pay-growth-outlook-unchanged-8d189e6b].

Gold has slipped to a three-week low at $4,584.40, down 1.95% on the session, as rising oil prices amplify hawkish central bank repricing, offsetting the metal's inflation-hedge appeal. Julius Baer analyst Carsten Menke noted: "The gold market seems more concerned by the risk of tighter monetary policy due to the impact of the war than the risk of slowing global growth." [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/gold-slips-to-three-week-low-as-oil-climbs-central-bank-decisions-loom.html]. Goldman Sachs has raised its Q4 Brent crude target to $90/bbl on "extreme" inventory draws — though current spot prices at $105 already significantly exceed those revised forecasts, suggesting the market is pricing in continued disruption well beyond consensus [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/goldman-hikes-oil-price-forecasts-on-extreme-inventory-draws].

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

**Iran War / Strait of Hormuz — Active & Escalating Risk**

- Iran offered a new proposal to the U.S. for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, conditional on Washington lifting its blockade and ending hostilities, while shelving nuclear talks; President Trump rejected the proposal, maintaining red lines including preventing Tehran from obtaining a nuclear weapon. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/27/us-iran-peace-talks-stall-global-markets-stocks-oil-treasurys.html]
- The Strait of Hormuz, which normally carries approximately one-fifth of global oil and gas shipments, remains almost entirely impassable, with the flow of oil and gas through the strait having "more or less stopped" per Lloyd's List data. [Source: https://www.icis.com/chemicals-and-the-economy/2026/04/imf-warns-on-inflation-and-recession-risks-as-iran-war-continues/]
- Goldman Sachs has cited "extreme" inventory draws — 255 million barrels since the conflict began — as the driver for raising Brent crude Q4 forecasts to $90/bbl, with spot prices already significantly higher. An industry analyst at Lipow Oil Associates estimates crude prices would drop $10/bbl immediately if conflict ended; absent progress, WTI is expected to drift back to $100 and Brent above $110. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/oil-prices-us-iran-hormuz-negotiations-wti-brent-crude.html]
- The IMF has formally warned on inflation and recession risks stemming from the conflict, noting oil and gas represent ~55% of global energy, versus ~15% for renewables, making the supply shock exceptionally disruptive. [Source: https://www.icis.com/chemicals-and-the-economy/2026/04/imf-warns-on-inflation-and-recession-risks-as-iran-war-continues/]
- Nearly 70% of UK construction firms fear "severe" impacts from the Middle East conflict over the next six months, including material cost inflation and supply chain disruptions. [Source: https://www.constructionnews.co.uk/consultants/middle-east-conflict-nearly-70-of-construction-firms-fear-severe-impact-28-04-2026/]
- Ray Dalio has warned the U.S. economy is entering a "stagflationary period," with the war described as a key structural catalyst. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/cnbc-daily-open-investors-look-past-warning-signs-to-send-stock-markets-soaring.html]

### Energy

**Brent Crude — ~$105.08** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- Brent is down 2.91% on the session but remains dramatically elevated, with near-term June contracts trading close to $111/bbl due to physical supply disruptions from the Hormuz closure. Goldman Sachs raised its Q4 Brent target to $90 on "extreme" inventory draws, but spot prices are already well above that level. [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/goldman-hikes-oil-price-forecasts-on-extreme-inventory-draws]

- Catalyst: Any signal from U.S.-Iran talks; Trump's maintained red lines on nuclear weapons keep the conflict risk premium elevated

**WTI Crude — ~$100.82** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- WTI surged +4.62% to $100.82, a divergence from Brent's decline reflecting roll dynamics and near-term physical market tightness. Lipow Oil Associates estimates WTI will drift back to $100 absent new negotiations — now effectively confirmed as a floor. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/oil-prices-us-iran-hormuz-negotiations-wti-brent-crude.html]
- Key levels: $94.00 support | $108.00 resistance
- Catalyst: U.S.-Iran negotiation progress (or lack thereof); OPEC+ production commentary this week

- European gas markets are facing tightening supply as longer sailing times mean the full impact of the Hormuz closure on European LNG imports is only now being felt. [Source: https://www.icis.com/explore/resources/news/2026/04/28/11201985/insight-global-ammonia-prices-to-stay-elevated-in-h2-26-to-fall-from-jan-27]

- Catalyst: LNG cargo re-routing from Middle East; European summer storage fill progress

**Henry Hub Natural Gas — ~$2.717** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- Henry Hub jumped +6.55% to $2.717, recovering after two down sessions, supported by cooler near-term weather, easing production, and solid LNG export demand. - Key levels: $2.50 support | $3.00 resistance
- Catalyst: Weekly EIA storage report; LNG export terminal capacity utilization

## 8. WEEK AHEAD / SCENARIO ANALYSIS

| SCENARIO | PROBABILITY | TRIGGER | BRENT | S&P 500 | BTC |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|---------|-----|
| Bull | 20% | Iran reopens Hormuz + Mag-7 earnings beat + FOMC signals H2 cuts | $88 | 7,500 | $86,000 |
| Base | 55% | FOMC holds hawkishly, Mag-7 earnings mixed, Iran talks stalled | $105 | 7,200 | $74,000 |
| Bear | 25% | Iran talks fully collapse + Fed more hawkish than expected + Mag-7 misses | $118 | 6,750 | $65,000 |

### Key Upcoming Catalysts (Week of April 28–May 2)

1. **Wednesday, April 30 — FOMC Rate Decision + Press Conference (Jerome Powell's final meeting):** Fed expected to hold at 3.50%-3.75%; tone on Iran-driven inflation vs. labour market weakness is the critical variable. Hawkish hold would lift USD, pressure gold and equities. [Source: https://www.cbsnews.com/news/fed-rate-decision-april-2026-powell-final-meeting/]

2. **Wednesday, April 30 — Mag-7 Earnings (Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft):** Unprecedented same-day reporting from four of the world's largest companies; combined $650bn AI capex guidance will be the key data point for Nasdaq direction. [Source: https://www.marketwatch.com/story/wall-streets-super-bowl-wednesday-alphabet-amazon-microsoft-and-meta-report-along-with-powells-last-fed-meeting-88eb1710]

3. **Wednesday, April 30 — Bank of Canada Rate Decision:** BoC expected to hold amid Iran war uncertainty; any dovish signal could weaken CAD vs. USD given oil market uncertainty. [Source: https://www.globalbankingandfinance.com/yen-steady-boj-kicks-off-big-week-central-banks/]

4. **Thursday, May 1 — ECB + BoE Rate Decisions:** Both expected to hold but with hawkish language on energy-driven inflation; Eurozone inflation expectations jumped sharply in March. ECB/BoE tone divergence will drive EUR/GBP and EUR/USD into the end of week. [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-26/bond-traders-eye-packed-week-of-rate-decisions-for-sell-signals]

5. **Ongoing — U.S.-Iran Strait of Hormuz Negotiations:** Any breakthrough (or breakdown) in talks represents the single highest-impact binary event for energy markets; Brent would fall approximately $10/bbl on ceasefire confirmation per industry estimates, and vice versa for escalation. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/oil-prices-us-iran-hormuz-negotiations-wti-brent-crude.html]

### Disputed

- [Report states the 10-year Treasury yield "rising to a three-week high" at 4.31%]: Partially misleading framing — the FRED data shows 4.31% as of April 24 (the most recent data point in the bundle), not confirmed as of today April 28. The CNBC gold article references a three-week high in 10yr yields consistent with this, but the report conflates a 4-day-old data point with intraday price action as if it were live.

- [Report characterizes ETH declining 1.14% as "underperforming Bitcoin on a relative basis"]: Technically disputed — ETH fell 1.14% vs. BTC's 1.43% decline; ETH actually outperformed Bitcoin on a percentage basis, not underperformed. The claim is directionally incorrect.

- [Report says "a divergence from Brent's decline" regarding WTI's +4.62% surge]: Partially misleading — both contracts are elevated versus pre-conflict levels. The WSJ notes Brent near-term June contracts (physical delivery) traded above $111/bbl while the July contract (reported as $105.08) was lower; the Brent-WTI divergence may be primarily a contract roll/delivery artifact rather than a fundamental divergence. The data bundle shows Brent (BZ=F) down 2.91% and WTI (CL=F) up 4.62% simultaneously, which is an unusually large spread meriting trader caution but the report does not flag the roll-date explanation.

- [Report describes the ECB and BoE as potentially signaling "openness to hikes" as a "material hawkish pivot from just months ago"]: The CNBC source cited states only that both banks will "stand pat" and "leave the door open to hikes later this year" — this characterization is present in the bundle, but labeling it a "material hawkish pivot" is an editorial amplification not directly supported by the source language.

- [Report cites gold June futures fell to $4,629.20 (CNBC source) while the data bundle shows spot/continuous gold (GC=F) at $4,584.40]: These are two different contract reference points ($44.80 apart). The report uses $4,584.40 as the headline price (from the bundle) but then cites $4,629.20 as supporting color — both are legitimate data points for different contracts, but the report does not clarify this distinction, which could mislead
